symptoms.symptom_name: Continuous burning pain out of proportion to the inciting injury

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome Type I
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Continuing pain disproportionate to the inciting event is a core Budapest sensory feature.
Pain out of proportion is also the earliest and most sensitive sign of compartment syndrome — cannot be used to exclude it.

symptoms.symptom_name: Mechanical allodynia (light touch of bedsheet/clothing provokes severe pain)

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome Type I
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Allodynia/hyperalgesia is a hallmark sensory abnormality in CRPS.
Prominent superficial cutaneous allodynia is atypical for deep venous pain, but does not exclude DVT.

symptoms.symptom_name: Unilateral oedema with vasomotor (mottled colour) and sudomotor (altered sweating) change

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome Type I
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Vasomotor and sudomotor asymmetry plus oedema are Budapest categories.
Unilateral swelling is a cardinal DVT feature — this overlap is why DVT must be actively excluded.
Redness and swelling overlap with cellulitis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Onset after minor tibial fracture with disproportionate progression

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome Type I
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: CRPS Type I characteristically follows a limb injury such as fracture.
Recent fracture/immobilisation is a major DVT risk factor.

symptoms.symptom_name: Dependent-position throbbing of the whole limb

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome Type I
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Dependent throbbing is non-specific and occurs with several oedematous/vascular limb conditions.

symptoms.symptom_name: No analgesic response to maximum-dose paracetamol and ibuprofen

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome Type I
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Neuropathic/central-sensitisation pain typically responds poorly to simple analgesics.

symptoms.symptom_name: Limb pallor, coldness, pulselessness or numbness

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Pallor, pulselessness and paraesthesia are limb-threatening ischaemia signs mandating emergency escalation.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome Type I
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A cold, pale, pulseless limb points away from CRPS and toward critical ischaemia.

symptoms.symptom_name: Palpable bilateral peripheral pulses; soft compartments; negative Homan's sign; no calf tenderness

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Soft compartments and intact pulses reduce the likelihood of compartment syndrome but do not fully exclude it.
Absent calf tenderness lowers suspicion but examination signs are unreliable to exclude DVT — imaging is required.

symptoms.symptom_name: Lives alone with high fall risk and cluttered home environment

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome Type I
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Not diagnostic, but a major safety/management modifier affecting disposition and choice of sedating medication.

symptoms.symptom_name: Absence of fever and of rapidly spreading erythema/red streaking

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of fever and spreading erythema, with pain out of proportion to erythema, argues against cellulitis.

symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome Type I
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Disproportionate burning pain with allodynia, vasomotor/sudomotor/oedema change following a limb fracture, unresponsive to simple analgesia, is a strong clinical pattern for CRPS Type I once mimics are excluded.

symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: Deep vein thrombosis / acute compartment syndrome
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: The same swollen, painful post-fracture limb is the classic setting for DVT and compartment syndrome; these must be excluded before accepting CRPS.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Objective exclusion of DVT in a swollen unilateral post-immobilisation limb; cannot be done via telehealth, so drives in-person referral.
Supports risk stratification for DVT alongside clinical probability; interpret with pre-test probability.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person limb assessment (pulses, compartments, calf, neurovascular status)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: The discriminating physical findings for compartment syndrome and DVT require hands-on examination that telehealth cannot provide.
Helps assess for infection if cellulitis remains a consideration.
May support CRPS diagnosis but is not part of the acute crisis workup and should not delay mimic exclusion — not to be ordered acutely from a telehealth encounter.
